current_turn_information: I gained 3 pounds overnight, despite taking double my normal torsemide dose.
I don’t have swelling in my legs or ankles,
I don’t have shortness of breath.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 57-year-old male with PMH of CAD s/p PCI LAD/LCx, HFrEF (EF 20%; unos 1B) s/p Heartware LVAD, complicated by LVAD hematoma s/p evacuation, endocarditis leading to AI and prosthetic AVR, IDDM, Addison's disease. Presented as direct admission from home for supratherapeutic INR and volume overload. Labs: INR 5.2, Hgb 7.6 from 8.5 on discharge, pro-BNP 1808, LDH 357, Cr 1.5. Repeat check: INR 3.6, Hct 22.9. Patient gained 3lbs overnight despite doubling torsemide. Recent complicated admission for sub-therapeutic INR and chronic decompensated heart failure. RHC showed elevated right and left-sided filling pressures. Diuresed with Lasix gtt and diuril boluses. Course c/b orthostasis and LVAD waveform dampening with standing, suggestive of RV dysfunction. Subsequently hypovolemic. Small IVF bolus improved milrinone gtt pressures but still suggestive of volume overload. Contradictory exam and pressures thought multifactorial: RV failure, possible pericardial constriction, kinked LVAD outflow tract. Deemed euvolemic and transitioned to Torsemide 80mg PO daily. Discharged off home amlodipine, on lower digoxin dose due to suspicion for digoxin toxicity. Fludricortistone dose increased from 0.1mg to 0.2mg daily. Currently denies F/C, HA, N/V/D, worsening cough, SOB, or CP. Admission physical: JVP 16cm, 2+ pitting edema extending to mid-shin. Discharge physical: no edema, fine bibasilar crackles, dry weight 106.4kg, discharge weight 106.8kg. Final Result: Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease with heart failure and stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease.

negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has CAD and reports weight gain without classic signs; do not use if imaging or labs are already available and conclusive, or if the patient has clear evidence of another cause.

current_turn_information: I gained an additional 3 pounds overnight, despite taking double my normal torsemide dose. I don’t know if this is recent weight gain or if my appetite has changed — I wasn’t told anything about appetite changes.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: Patient is a 57-year-old male with PMH of CAD s/p PCI LAD/LCx, HFrEF (EF 20%; unos 1B) s/p Heartware LVAD in ___ ___omplicated by LVAD hematoma s/p evacuation in ___, endocarditis leading to AI and prosthetic AVR, IDDM, and Addison's disease. Presented as direct admission from home for supratherapeutic INR and volume overload. Labs notable for INR 5.2, Hgb 7.6 from 8.5 on discharge, pro-BNP 1808, LDH 357, Cr 1.5. On repeat check morning of ___, INR was reportedly 3.6 and Hct 22.9. Patient had gained 3lbs overnight despite double torsemide dose. Recent complicated admission for sub-therapeutic INR and chronic decompensated heart failure. Underwent RHC with elevated right and left-sided filling pressures. Diuresed with Lasix gtt and intermittent diuril boluses. Course c/b orthostasis and LVAD waveform dampening with standing, suggestive of RV dysfunction. Subsequently hypovolemic, received small IVF bolus, milrinone gtt pressures improved but still suggestive of volume overload. Contradictory exam and pressures thought multifactorial (RV failure, pericardial constriction, kinked LVAD outflow tract). Deemed euvolemic, transitioned to Torsemide 80mg PO daily on discharge. Discharged off home amlodipine, on lower digoxin dose due to suspicion for digoxin toxicity. Upon arrival, patient endorsed history, torsemide decreased from 80mg BID to 80mg daily, fludricortistone increased from 0.1mg to 0.2mg daily. Currently denies F/C, HA, N/V/D, worsening cough, SOB or CP. Admission physical: JVP 16cm, 2+ pitting edema extending to mid-shin. Discharge physical: no edema, dry weight 106.4kg, discharge weight 106.8kg. Final result: Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease with heart failure and stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease.

Presented as direct admission from home for supratherapeutic INR and volume overload. Labs: INR 5.2, Hgb 7.6 from 8.5 on discharge, pro-BNP 1808, LDH 357, Cr 1.5. Repeat check: INR 3.6, Hct 22.9. Gained 3lbs overnight despite double torsemide dose (80mg BID). Recent complicated admission for sub-therapeutic INR and chronic decompensated heart failure. RHC showed elevated right and left-sided filling pressures. Diuresed with Lasix gtt and diuril boluses. Course c/b orthostasis and LVAD waveform dampening with standing, suggestive of RV dysfunction. Subsequently hypovolemic, improved with IVF and milrinone gtt. Contradictory exam and pressures thought multifactorial (RV failure, pericardial constriction, kinked LVAD outflow tract). Deemed euvolemic, transitioned to Torsemide 80mg PO daily. Discharged off home amlodipine, lower digoxin dose (suspicion for toxicity). Fludricortistone dose increased from 0.1mg to 0.2mg daily. Currently denies F/C, HA, N/V/D, worsening cough, SOB, or CP. Admitted 2164-12-30 15:57:00, discharged 2165-01-09 17:15:00. Admission weight 106.4kg, discharge weight 106.8kg. Physical exam: JVP 16cm, 2+ pitting edema in ___ extending to mid-shin. Discharge exam: no edema, JVP 0 at 90 degrees. Final Result: Hypertensive heart and chronic kidney disease with heart failure and stage 1 through stage 4 chronic kidney disease, or unspecified chronic kidney disease.

query.query_text: Volume overload with new symptoms of orthostatic dizziness and palpitations; need memory about evaluating for hemodynamic instability or LVAD dysfunction; avoid premature diagnosis without imaging or lab correlation

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with redness, swelling, and pus around a nail, and the history suggests recent trauma or nail-biting, but no imaging or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Update the differential to include paronychia and confirm the diagnosis with targeted patient history about trauma or nail manipulation, then finalize only after ruling out onychia or other nail infections.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms suggest paronychia and no imaging or labs are available; do not use if there is evidence of nail bed involvement or if the patient has systemic signs of infection.

positive_experience.situation: When hip pain is described as aching and localized with stiffness and functional limitation, but no imaging or labs are available, what should the doctor avoid?
positive_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without imaging or physical exam findings, as this may lead to premature closure and miss alternative diagnoses like infection or fracture.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when imaging or physical exam is missing; do not use if imaging is available and shows clear osteoarthritis or if the patient has systemic symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.

positive_experience.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
positive_experience.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, abdominal cramping, dizziness, and syncope, especially with weight loss and low-grade fever, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without ruling out mechanical or structural causes.
negative_experience.action: Do not finalize diagnosis without requesting imaging or performing a focused physical exam to assess for signs of fracture or musculoskeletal injury, especially given the mechanism of syncope during straining.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has a history of trauma or mechanical symptoms, and imaging or physical exam is not yet performed; do not use if imaging is available and shows no fracture or if the patient has clear systemic signs of cancer.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with recurrent wheezing, shortness of breath, and cough triggered by allergens or viral infections, and has a history of nasal congestion, but no imaging or lab results are available, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without ruling out aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease.
negative_experience.action: Do not finalize asthma diagnosis without specifically investigating for aspirin sensitivity or nasal polyps, especially when symptoms are triggered by environmental exposures or viral infections.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms suggest asthma but no imaging or lab data is available; do not use if nasal polyps or aspirin sensitivity history is confirmed or if imaging shows structural abnormalities.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with fatigue and cough, and initial history suggests no fever or night sweats, but the final diagnosis is MDS while the gold diagnosis is viral pneumonia, the doctor should avoid premature finalization without ruling out infectious causes.
negative_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without reviewing imaging or lab results that could confirm or rule out viral pneumonia.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has fatigue and cough with no fever or night sweats, but only if imaging or lab results are unavailable or not reviewed; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and show signs of infection.

current_turn_information: I’ve noticed I’ve been gaining weight — my wife said I gained 3 pounds overnight.
